revision.text: #redirect[[en:Red_Spring]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
#REDIRECT[[赤湯温泉]]

'''赤湯'''（あかゆ）は、湯の色が[[赤色]]をした[[温泉]]の総称。[[濁り湯]]の一種である。
== 概要 == 浴槽内で析出、または沈殿している[[湯の花]]の色が赤色をしている湯に対してこの名称がつけられることが多い。赤色をしているのは、温泉成分に含まれる鉄分が酸化鉄として湯の花になるためである。著名な赤湯は[[有馬温泉]]の金泉である。
赤色の湯は特徴的であることから、赤色の湯はそのまま温泉名も「赤湯」となることが多い。しかしながら[[山形県]]の[[赤湯温泉 (山形県)|赤湯温泉]]のように無色透明の湯も存在する。これは[[開湯伝説]]に因んで命名された湯であるためである。
== 関連項目 == {{ウィキポータルリンク|温泉}} * [[温泉]]、[[温泉街]]、[[外湯]]、[[温泉番付]] * [[日本の温泉地一覧]] - [[日本の温泉画像一覧]]
[[Category:温泉|あかゆ]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses|red-colored hot springs|various Akayu Onsen in Japan|赤湯温泉}} {{Notability|date=August 2011}} {{Citations needed|date=November 2011}} [[File:141108 Gekkoen Yugetsusanso Arima Onsen Kobe Japan04bs3.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}} Kinsen]] [[File:Beppu Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Chinoike Jigoku|ja|血の池地獄}} at {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|柴石温泉}}]] '''Akayu''' (赤湯) is a general term for hot springs ({{ill|Onsen|ja|温泉}}) with red-colored water. It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯}}.
== Overview == The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}.
The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." However, not all Akayu are red; for instance, the {{ill|Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|ja|赤湯温泉 (山形県)}} in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} features clear, colorless water. This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Legends|ja|開湯伝説}} rather than the water's appearance.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Ferruginous Spring|ja|含鉄泉|1=Iron-containing springs}}/{{ill|Copper-Iron Spring|ja|含銅-鉄泉|1=Copper-Iron Spring}} * {{ill|Oxidation|ja|酸化}}
{{onsen-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:赤湯]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11635790]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Notability|date=August 2011}}
{{Citations needed|date=November 2011}}
[[File:141108 Gekkoen Yugetsusanso Arima Onsen Kobe Japan04bs3.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] Kinsen]]
[[File:Beppu Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Chinoike Jigoku|ja|血の池地獄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinoike Jigoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府地獄めぐり|1=Hells of Beppu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5709027}} at {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|柴石温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]]
'''Akayu''' (赤湯) is a general term for hot springs ([[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]) with red-colored water. It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murky Hot Springs|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]].

The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." '''Akayu''' (赤湯) is a general term for hot springs ([[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]) with red-colored water. It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murky Hot Springs|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]].

The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." However, not all Akayu are red; for instance, the {{ill|Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|ja|赤湯温泉 (山形県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|1=Akayu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17238816}} in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] features clear, colorless water. This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}} rather than the water's appearance.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11635790]]

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Notability|date=August 2011}}
{{Citations needed|date=November 2011}}
[[File:141108 Gekkoen Yugetsusanso Arima Onsen Kobe Japan04bs3.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] Kinsen]]
[[File:Beppu Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Chinoike Jigoku|ja|血の池地獄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinoike Jigoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府地獄めぐり|1=Hells of Beppu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5709027}} at {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|柴石温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]]
'''Akayu''' (赤湯) is a general term for hot springs ([[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]) with red-colored water. It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murky Hot Springs|qq=}}.

== Overview ==
The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]].

The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." However, not all Akayu are red; for instance, the {{ill|Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|ja|赤湯温泉 (山形県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|1=Akayu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17238816}} in [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] features clear, colorless water. This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}} rather than the water's appearance.

== Related Articles ==
* [[:en:Chalybeate|Chalybeate springs]]/{{ill|Copper-Iron Spring|ja|含銅-鉄泉|1=Copper-Iron Spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Copper-Iron Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Copper-Iron Spring|qq=}}
* {{ill|Oxidation|ja|酸化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oxidation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=oxidation|WD=Q1786087}}

This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}} rather than the water's appearance.

It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murky Hot Springs|qq=}}.
== Overview == The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}}.
The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." However, not all Akayu are red; for instance, the {{ill|Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|ja|赤湯温泉 (山形県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|1=Akayu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17238816}} in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} features clear, colorless water. This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}} rather than the water's appearance.

{{Notability|date=August 2011}} {{Citations needed|date=November 2011}} [[File:141108 Gekkoen Yugetsusanso Arima Onsen Kobe Japan04bs3.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen|1=Arima Onsen}} Kinsen]] [[File:Beppu Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Chinoike Jigoku|ja|血の池地獄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinoike Jigoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府地獄めぐり|1=Hells of Beppu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5709027}} at {{ill|Beppu Onsen|ja|柴石温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別府温泉|1=Beppu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4890932}}]] '''Akayu''' (赤湯) is a general term for hot springs ({{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}) with red-colored water. It is a type of {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murky Hot Springs|qq=}}.
== Overview == The term "Akayu" is often used to describe hot springs where the {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}, which precipitate or settle in the bath, are red in color. This red hue is typically due to the high iron content in the hot spring water, which oxidizes to form iron oxide deposits. A well-known example of Akayu is the Kinsen ("Golden Spring") of {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen|1=Arima Onsen}}.
The distinctive red color often influences the naming of hot springs as "Akayu." However, not all Akayu are red; for instance, the {{ill|Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|ja|赤湯温泉 (山形県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akayu Onsen (Yamagata)|1=Akayu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17238816}} in {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} features clear, colorless water. This name was derived from local {{ill|Onsen Origin Myth|ja|開湯伝説|1=Onsen Origin Myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Origin Myth|qq=}} rather than the water's appearance.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Chalybeate|en|Chalybeate|WD=Q3046709|lt=Chalybeate springs|1=Chalybeate springs}}/{{ill|Copper-Iron Spring|ja|含銅-鉄泉|1=Copper-Iron Spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Copper-Iron Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Copper-Iron Spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Oxidation|ja|酸化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oxidation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Oxidation|WD=Q1786087}}
